source_title: Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis on AI, Creativity, and a Golden Age of Science | All-In Summit

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: First off, congrats on winning the Nobel Prize for the incredible breakthrough of AlphaFold. Everyone here would love to hear where you were when you found out.

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: It's a very surreal moment. They tell you like 10 minutes before it all goes live. You can't really—you're sort of shell-shocked when you get that call from Sweden. It's a call that every scientist dreams about. The most amazing bit is they bring out this Nobel book from the vaults in the safe, and you get to sign your name next to all the other greats. Leafing back to the other pages and seeing Feynman and Marie Curie and Einstein and Niels Bohr, and you just carry on going backwards and you get to put your name in that book. It's incredible.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: What is DeepMind within Alphabet? What are you responsible for?

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: We sort of merged a couple of years back all of the different AI efforts across Google and Alphabet into one division. The way I describe it now is that we're the engine room of the whole of Google and the whole of Alphabet. Gemini, our main model that we're building, but also many of the other models—the video models and interactive world models—we plug them in all across Google now. So every product, every surface area has one of our AI models in it. Billions of people now interact with Gemini models, whether that's through AI overview, AI mode, or the Gemini app. There's around 5,000 people in my org, predominantly 80% plus engineers and PhD researchers.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: You released this Genie World model. What is it?

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: All of these videos, all these interactive worlds you're seeing—you're seeing someone actually control the video. It's not static video. It's just being generated by a text prompt. People are able to control the 3D environment using the arrow keys and the spacebar. Everything you're seeing here is being fully—all these pixels are being generated on the fly. They don't exist until the player or the person interacting with it goes to that part of the world. This model is reverse engineering intuitive physics. It's watched many millions of videos and YouTube videos about the world, and just from that, it's kind of reverse engineered how a lot of the world works. It can generate a consistent minute or two of interaction as you as the user in many, many different worlds.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: Where does this lead us? Fast forward this model to Gen 5.

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: For an AI to be truly general, to build AGI, we feel that the AGI system needs to understand the world around us and the physical world around us, not just the abstract world of languages or mathematics. That's what's critical for robotics to work. It's probably what's missing from it today. And also things like smart glasses, a smart glasses system that helps you in your everyday life. It's got to understand the physical context that you're in and how the intuitive physics of the world works.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: What is the state of the art with the vision, language, action models today? A generalized system that can observe the world with a camera, and I can use language to tell it what I want you to do, and then it knows how to act physically.

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: We've built something called Gemini robotics models, which are sort of fine-tuned Gemini with extra robotics data. We've got these tabletop setups of two hands interacting with objects on a table, two robotic hands. You can just talk to the robot. You can say, put the yellow object into the red bucket, or whatever it is. And it will interpret that instruction, that language instruction, into motor movements. That's the power of a multimodal model—it will be able to bring in real-world understanding to the way you interact with it.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: Does that mean that ultimately you could build what would be the equivalent of an operating system layer, or like an Android, for generalized robotics?

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: Exactly. That's certainly one strategy we're pursuing, is a kind of Android play as a kind of robotics, almost an OS layer, cross-robotics. But there's also some quite interesting things about vertically integrating our latest models with very specific robot types and robot designs, and some kind of end-to-end learning of that, too. Both are actually pretty interesting, and we're pursuing both strategies.

paragraphs.speaker: host
paragraphs.name: Host
paragraphs.text: Let's talk about applications in science. What are the areas of science and breakthroughs that you're most excited about?

paragraphs.speaker: demis
paragraphs.name: Demis Hassabis
paragraphs.text: AI to accelerate scientific discovery and help with things like human health is the reason I spent my whole career on AI.
